title: SEGMENT 15 — Advisory committee meetings and post-market commitments

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Advisory committee meetings and post-market commitments
I want to spend this segment on two related features of the regulatory landscape that often get less attention than they should: advisory committee meetings and post-market commitments.
Advisory committees are panels of outside experts — clinicians, statisticians, patient representatives, ethicists — that FDA convenes to provide independent input on specific drug applications.

For gene therapies, the relevant advisory committees are the Oncologic Drugs Advisory Committee — ODAC — for cancer-related products, and the Cellular, Tissue, and Gene Therapies Advisory Committee — CTGTAC — for cell and gene therapies specifically.
The advisory committee meeting is, structurally, a public event.
The committee members review the application materials in advance.
The sponsor presents the data.
The FDA presents its analysis.
Patients and patient advocates often present testimony.
The committee then deliberates in public — often for hours, with substantive disagreement among members — and at the end takes a formal vote on specific questions posed by FDA.
The questions might be: does the data support approval?
Is the benefit-risk profile favorable?
Are the post-market commitments adequate?
Let me give you the analogy.
The advisory committee is the public hearing.

Imagine a court case in which the prosecutor — the FDA — and the defense — the sponsor — both present their evidence in front of a jury, but the jury is made up of outside experts in the technical field, and the deliberations happen in front of an audience that includes the press, patients, advocates, and academic observers.

The jury's verdict is advisory only — FDA is not bound by the committee's vote — but in practice, FDA usually follows the committee's recommendation, and the public deliberation itself shapes the eventual regulatory decision in subtle ways.
The questions the committee members ask.
The concerns they raise.
The voices of patients in the room.
All of it feeds back into the agency's thinking.
There have been famous and consequential advisory committee meetings for gene therapies.
The Luxturna advisory committee — which was actually CTGTAC's predecessor — voted unanimously in favor of approval in 2017, on the basis of the dramatic clinical data.
The Casgevy advisory committee in 2023 was more contentious, with substantial discussion of long-term safety risks of CRISPR editing — what about off-target edits we haven't found yet?
what about the unknown effects fifteen years from now?
— and the committee ultimately recommended approval but with attached recommendations for extensive post-market follow-up.
Those recommendations directly shaped the post-market commitments that Vertex agreed to as a condition of approval.
Now let me talk about post-market commitments specifically, because for gene therapy they are uniquely intense.
When an AAV gene therapy is approved, the FDA generally requires the sponsor to commit to long-term follow-up of treated patients for up to fifteen years.
Fifteen years.
That is the longest post-market follow-up commitment in modern medicine for any class of product.

The reasoning is that a one-time vector administration could have effects — particularly late-emerging adverse events related to vector integration, or sustained immune responses, or the durability of expression — that simply cannot be characterized in a clinical trial of finite duration.
The fifteen-year window is meant to capture those late effects if they occur.

Practically, the commitment takes the form of a patient registry — a structured database that tracks every patient who received the therapy, captures their clinical outcomes at regular follow-up intervals, and reports back to FDA.
The Zolgensma RESTORE registry I mentioned in Segment 12 is the model.
Hemgenix has a similar registry.
Casgevy and Lyfgenia have registries.
Every approved gene therapy carries a registry obligation, and those registries are now an enormous body of post-market data that the field as a whole learns from.
The registries are not just for safety.
They also generate the durability data that the field desperately needs.
Does Zolgensma's effect last five years?
Ten?
Permanent?
Does Hemgenix's factor IX expression remain at clinically meaningful levels a decade after dosing?
These are questions you can only answer by following real patients for real time.
The registries, awkwardly and slowly, are answering them.
For a sponsor, the post-market commitments are a real ongoing obligation.
You don't get to declare victory at approval and walk away.
You have to staff the registry.
You have to keep contact with patients and treating clinicians.
You have to publish the data periodically.
You have to report adverse events as they emerge.
You have to be ready, fifteen years out from approval, to address whatever the data shows.
Approval is not the finish line.
It is the start of a fifteen-year continuing engagement.
This is, in some ways, the right design.
Gene therapy is a fundamentally new modality, and we genuinely do not yet know the long-term consequences of single-dose vector administration.
The registries are how we will learn.
They are also a real cost of doing business in this field, and they shape how sponsors plan their commercial strategy.
